Q1: I organized my study routine around blocks of time between my part-time job and other responsibilities. Since I don't have a lot of resources, I had to be strategic about when I could access reliable internet at the library or campus. I typically dedicated about three hours a day to studying, spread across mornings before work and evenings when I could. I'm a visual learner, so I spent a lot of time creating diagrams and color-coded notes that helped me retain information better.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on deadlines and what was happening in my life. Some weeks I could stick to a regular morning study schedule, but other weeks when work picked up extra shifts or assignments piled up, I had to be more flexible. I tried to maintain consistency, but honestly, deadlines often dictated when I studied most intensely. I learned to adapt quickly because that's just how my life works.
Q3: Usually I logged into the LMS to check for new announcements or upcoming deadlines. Sometimes it was to watch lecture videos or download materials I needed for the week. The gamified tasks actually made me curious to log in more often to see my progress and earn those little badges. It gave me a small sense of accomplishment even when things felt overwhelming.
Q4: Yes, checking the LMS became part of my morning routine, usually with my first cup of coffee. I'd quickly scan for any updates before heading to work or class. The gamified elements helped make it feel less like a chore and more like checking in on something I was building. It took a few weeks, but eventually it just became automatic.
Q5: The lecture videos were incredibly useful because I could pause, rewind, and take visual notes at my own pace. I watched almost 200 minutes of video content because that format really clicks with my learning style. The gamified tasks were surprisingly helpful too—they broke down big concepts into smaller challenges that felt manageable. Having those visual progress indicators kept me motivated.
Q6: I basically ignored the discussion forums entirely. I know I should have participated, but with limited time and internet access, typing out thoughtful responses felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. I also minimized my use of supplementary readings when I was short on time. It wasn't that they weren't valuable, but I had to prioritize what would help me pass.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to the assignments or exams. If I could immediately apply what I learned to something I'd be graded on, that was gold. The gamified tasks were great because they gave instant feedback and showed me exactly where I stood. Anything that felt abstract or disconnected from assessments got pushed to the back burner.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—they were like bonuses I could pursue if I had extra time and energy. Required activities had this weight to them that made me prioritize them no matter what. With optional stuff, I'd often think 'that would be nice' but then life would get in the way. The gamified optional tasks were an exception though; they were designed to feel rewarding rather than burdensome.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture slides and videos when I had good internet access, then study them later at home or during breaks at work. I filled notebooks with hand-drawn diagrams and visual summaries because that's how information sticks for me. I also used free YouTube tutorials and open-source textbooks to supplement what we covered in class.
Q10: There were many times I was deeply engaged with course material but the LMS wouldn't show it. I'd spend hours redrawing concept maps from memory or quizzing myself using flashcards I made. Sometimes I'd study with a classmate in person, working through problems together without ever touching the LMS. My best learning often happened with a pen and paper, far from any screen.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd access a lecture video once, download it or take detailed notes, and then never return to that specific LMS page again. My notes and downloaded files became my primary study materials. It was more efficient for me and didn't depend on having consistent internet access.
Q12: There were definitely harder moments, especially around midterms when work scheduled me for extra shifts and I had multiple assignments due. I remember feeling surprised by how quickly everything piled up at certain points. My stress levels would spike, but I've learned to push through those moments rather than shut down. I just had to remind myself that difficult periods are temporary.
Q13: Workload and competing responsibilities affected my LMS use significantly. When I was exhausted from work, I'd sometimes just skim materials instead of engaging deeply. Confusion about certain topics made me spend more time on videos but less time on other activities. I had to constantly triage what deserved my limited time and energy, which meant the LMS often got the minimum viable attention.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd have a video playing while my mind was completely elsewhere, worrying about bills or my next work shift. I'd technically be 'watching' but absorbing nothing. Sometimes I'd scroll through readings without really processing them because I was too tired. Those moments felt frustrating because I knew I was wasting precious time.
Q15: I definitely postponed discussion posts because they felt like they required more mental energy than I had available. I knew participating could help my understanding, but the barrier felt too high when I was managing everything else. Sometimes I'd avoid starting a difficult assignment because I was afraid of not having enough time to do it well. I'd rather wait until I could give it proper attention.
Q16: Deadlines were my primary driver for LMS activity. I'd plan backwards from due dates, figuring out what I needed to access and when. My login frequency probably spiked dramatically in the days before major deadlines. The gamified tasks helped spread things out a bit, but honestly, without deadlines I might have been even less active on the platform.
Q17: Feedback definitely influenced my engagement. When I received constructive comments on assignments, I'd go back and review related materials to improve. Positive feedback on the gamified tasks gave me little boosts of motivation to keep going. However, when feedback was delayed or vague, I felt less connected to the course and less sure about where to focus my efforts.
Q18: The course structure with its 13 online modules gave me flexibility, which was essential for my situation. I could work ahead during lighter weeks and catch up during busier ones. The gamified elements added a layer of structure that helped me stay on track without feeling micromanaged. Having clear milestones made a huge difference in keeping me organized.
Q19: The gamified task design made engagement easier because it turned progress into something visible and rewarding. However, the discussion requirements were harder for me because they demanded synchronous-style participation that didn't fit my schedule. The video-heavy format worked well for my visual learning style. More flexibility in how we demonstrated participation would have helped.
Q20: I think students might show high activity because they're searching for understanding but struggling to produce quality work. Maybe they're watching videos repeatedly or reading materials multiple times without it clicking. It could also be anxiety-driven behavior—feeling like you need to check everything constantly but being paralyzed when it comes to actually submitting work. Or they might be dealing with external pressures that fragment their attention.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience to some extent. I performed reasonably well despite relatively low engagement metrics because I studied efficiently offline. My high attendance and the work I did outside the LMS contributed more to my learning than my login frequency would suggest. Sometimes quality of engagement matters more than quantity of clicks.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns of access, timing around deadlines, and which resources students gravitate toward. But they completely miss the depth of offline engagement—the notebooks filled with notes, the study groups, the mental processing that happens away from screens. They also can't capture whether someone was truly focused or just going through the motions while logged in.
Q23: Being engaged means actively wrestling with the material until it makes sense, not just passively consuming it. For me, it's about creating those visual connections and being able to explain concepts in my own words. It's showing up mentally even when circumstances make it difficult, and finding ways to learn that work for your situation. True engagement is internal—it's caring about understanding, not just completing.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I overcame to stay engaged—the financial stress, the work schedule, the limited resources. It wouldn't capture the resilience it took to keep pushing forward when things got hard or the creative strategies I developed to learn effectively despite constraints. My zero discussion posts might look like disengagement, but really I was channeling my limited energy into what would help me pass. The data misses the whole human story behind the numbers.
